preamble: The transformation of the voiced labio-velar approximant /w/ to the voiceless labiodental fricative /f/ is a phonetic shift that involves both a strengthening of articulation (fortition) and a loss of voicing (devoicing), accompanied by a shift in the place of articulation from labio-velar to purely labiodental.
phoneticEffects: Fortition (approximant to fricative), Devoicing, Place Assimilation (loss of velar component, becoming labiodental)

examples.note: Proto-Celtic *w developed into /f/ in Old Irish. This is evident in the f-future tense and in the historical reconstruction of Ogam symbols.

sources: McManus, D. (1991). A Guide to Ogam. Maynooth Monographs 4. An Sagart.
Schumacher, S. (2004). The Old Irish f-future. In J. H. W. Penney (Ed.), Indo-European Perspectives: Studies in Honour of Anna Morpurgo Davies (pp. 477-490). Oxford University Press.
Stifter, D. (2006). Sengoídelc: Old Irish for Beginners. Syracuse University Press.
Woolf, A. (2007). From Pictland to Alba 789–1070. Edinburgh University Press.
